Dissertation Title: BLOCKCHAIN-BASED FRAUD DETECTION USING 
GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS(GNN’s) 
 
Master title: MSC DATA ANALYTICS 
 
Name: WILLIAM KOJO HOTOR 
 
Year: 2024/25

2 
ABSTRACT 
 
The current study examines the limitations of conventional anti-fraud techniques applied to 
blockchain finance and suggests the use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to support the 
detection of fraudulent transactions. As decentralized finance platforms expand, traditional rule 
and machine learning approaches are unable to model the relational complexity of blockchain 
data. To fill this gap, the study seeks to develop, deploy, and test GNN structures—i.e., Graph 
Convolutional Network (GCN), Graph Attention Network (GAT), and GraphSAGE—to detect 
fraudulent transactions in blockchain transaction graphs. A mixed-methods strategy was used. 
Primary data were procured through 50-participant surveys to measure user perception and 
awareness of GNN-based fraudulent detection. Secondary data analysis included the use of 
Elliptic Bitcoin Transaction Dataset, consisting of more than 46,000 transactions and 166 
features per node. All the GNN models were compared against conventional approaches 
including Logistic Regression, Isolation Forest, and Autoencoders. The findings showed 
GraphSAGE to outstrip all the rest of the models with 95.08% accuracy and 0.9540 weighted F1-
score. The conventional models, and particularly the unsupervised ones, performed poorly with 
high false positives or did not detect the fraud at all. The results of the surveys showed that there 
existed a high correlation between GNN efficacy and awareness of technology.  
The study substantiates the fact that GNNs provide an accurate, context-aware, and scalable 
solution to blockchain fraud detection. The study further emphasizes the need to educate users to 
drive the adoption of AI-powered security technologies in decentralized finance systems. 
Keywords: Blockchain, Fraud Detection, Graph Neural Networks, GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, 
DeFi, Bitcoin, Machine Learning, Anomaly Detection.

INTRODUCTION 
 
 
1.1 Research Background 
Blockchain technology has been hailed as a revolutionary concept in the digital age that was first 
designed to underpin decentralized cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin and Ethereum. With time, its 
distributed, tamper-proof, and transparent framework has paved the way for use in industries 
from medicine to transport, and most importantly, finance. The transformation of blockchain 
technology into a platform for decentralized finance (DeFi) has made it possible for 
intermediary-free transactions between two parties, enabling increased use and acceptance in 
finance (Ashfaq, et al., 2022). The same features that facilitate DeFi come with certain 
peculiarities—especially in detecting and stopping fraud. 
Decentralized platforms function outside of centralized control structures, and thus conventional 
control and monitoring methods do not apply. Contrary to centralized banks that have highly 
regulated and monitored transactions, blockchain systems have been based on distributed 
consensus and pseudonymity that renders tracing and identification of suspicious activity 
difficult. Consequently, financial fraud in its forms from Ponzi schemes and money laundering 
through phishing and exploitation of smart contracts has become highly sophisticated. 
Traditional fraud detection systems based on rule-based algorithmic approaches and traditional 
machine learning methods are not conducive to capturing the relational and dynamic 
environment of blockchain data. These systems tend to depend on strict rule sets and table-like 
data representations in a way that constrains their ability to recognize intricate patterns of 
transactions. Blockchain transactions naturally take the form of graph-like structures in which 
wallet addresses are nodes and transactions are edges. 
Graph-based machine learning via Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) provides an innovative 
solution for tackling this challenge. GNNs are capable of learning from both node features and 
edges and are particularly suitable for pattern detection in blockchain networks. Recent research 
has shown that GNNs outperform other models in tasks including anomaly detection and 
relational learning (Chen, et al., 2025). Therefore, in this research, we examine the possibility for

12
GNNs in improving fraud detection in blockchain transactions in filling an essential void in 
detection methods. 
1.2 Problem Statement 
Even though blockchain has its inherent openness, fraud is an omnipresent threat. The anonymity 
and decentralization in blockchain technology facilitate malicious players in making use of 
loopholes. Fraudsters can conceal origin and movement of illegal funds through multiple-layered 
transactions that become challenging for current systems to detect. 
Traditional methods of fraud detection are inadequate in this instance. Static thresholds and 
hand-coded features for rule-based systems are not capable of detecting dynamic fraud patterns. 
Also inadequate are conventional machine learning models that take individual transactions in 
isolation while ignoring the network of interactions and relationships that underpin blockchain 
activity. The result is high false positives as well as missed detections. 
In addition, larger blockchain networks make it even more challenging. With millions of 
transactions being executed on thousands of wallets, detection of suspicious patterns becomes 
computationally expensive (Luo, et al., 2024). The absence of labeled datasets also renders 
supervised learning models ineffective in performing in practical environments. 
There thus exists an urgent need for sophisticated, scalable, and relationally-aware fraud 
detection systems. The current research responds to this need through its use of Graph Neural 
Networks that can examine graphs of transactions and detect anomalies in these graphs' 
structure. Through doing this, the research hopes to enhance fraud detection accuracy and 
reliability in decentralized finance networks. 
1.3 Research Aim 
The primary aim of this research is to design, implement, and evaluate a graph-based fraud 
detection system using Graph Neural Networks for blockchain transaction networks. The study 
intends to demonstrate how GNN architectures such as Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN), 
Graph Attention Networks (GAT), and GraphSAGE can outperform traditional fraud detection 
models in identifying illicit blockchain activities.

13
1.4 Research Objectives 
To support the overall aim, the following objectives have been established: 
1. To critically assess the limitations of current fraud detection techniques in blockchain-
based financial systems. 
2. To design and implement Graph Neural Network (GNN) architectures suitable for fraud 
detection in blockchain transaction graphs. 
3. To compare the performance of GNN-based models with traditional machine learning 
models in terms of accuracy, recall, and computational efficiency. 
4. To evaluate user awareness and perception of GNN-based fraud detection mechanisms 
through primary survey-based research. 
 
1.5 Research Questions 
The research is driven by the following core questions: 
1. What are the limitations of existing fraud detection methods in the context of 
decentralized finance? 
2. How effectively can Graph Neural Networks detect fraudulent transactions in blockchain 
networks compared to traditional models? 
3. What role does user awareness play in the perception of GNN-based fraud detection 
effectiveness? 
1.6 Research Hypotheses 
Based on a combination of literature review and preliminary primary data analysis, the study is 
guided by the following hypotheses: 
 
H1: Participants with higher familiarity with blockchain are more likely to trust DeFi 
platforms to be secure from fraud.

14
 
H2: Users who have encountered fraudulent crypto transactions exhibit lower confidence 
in identifying future fraud. 
 
H3: Awareness of blockchain’s graph structure correlates with belief in the effectiveness 
of GNNs for fraud detection. 
1.7 Significance of the Study 
This study is important from both practical and theoretical perspectives. Theoretically, it 
contributes to blockchain analytics and fraud detection by applying sophisticated graph learning 
methods. The study provides a new method for analyzing decentralized transactions using 
GNNs—a topic still not fully explored in literature. 
Practically speaking, the research provides applied relevance for financial institutions, DeFi 
platforms, and regulators. By suggesting a context-aware and scalable detection framework, it 
improves stakeholders' capability for fraud detection and prevention (Firdaus, and Sumardi, 
2024). Strategically, it helps grow trust and integrity in decentralized financial systems, which 
would help in widespread adoption. 
In addition, the research connects technical innovation and user acceptance. Through feedback 
from surveys incorporated in the study, it provides context for AI-based fraud detection systems 
in real settings by tackling technical feasibility, as well as social acceptability. 
1.8 Scope and Delimitations 
This research narrows its scope to fraud detection within Bitcoin blockchain networks through 
the use of Graph Neural Networks in conjunction with the Elliptic dataset for secondary data 
collection. The data set offers labeled transactions in the form of licit, illicit, and unknown 
classifications from which models for both supervised and semi-supervised learning are based. 
Though findings are generalizable to other cryptocurrencies or blockchain platforms in theory, 
Bitcoin is primarily involved because of available data. 
Furthermore, the study is augmented by primary data gathered from a structured Google Forms 
survey of 50 respondents. The participant base has been kept small and exploratory in nature in 
an effort to glean qualitative findings about what people perceive about Online rather than 
making absolute statistical inferences.

15
The research does not tackle real-time deployment requirements or hardware optimization for 
GNNs but discusses scalability and model performance from a theoretical point of view. 
1.9 Methodological Approach 
The research employs an organized quantitative research methodology, making use of both 
primary and secondary data in exploring fraud detection in blockchain networks. The primary 
data were gathered using a well-formulated questionnaire for determining user perceptions 
towards blockchain use, fraud exposure, and detection using AI-based methods. Secondary data 
from the Elliptic Bitcoin Transaction Dataset were reorganized into graph structures for analysis 
purposes. Graph Neural Networks -GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE were employed in node 
classification, and results were benchmarked using conventional models. This research approach 
facilitates the study's hypotheses and gives a solid framework for discovering illicit blockchain 
transactions through relational learning. 
 
1.10 Structure of the Dissertation 
The dissertation has five chapters. After this chapter of introduction, Chapter Two gives an 
extensive literature survey describing conventional fraud detection processes, graph analytics, 
and GNNs in blockchain contexts. Chapter Three describes research methodology involving data 
sources, system models, as well as evaluation criteria. Chapter Four documents and analyses 
results from primary and secondary data. Lastly, Chapter Five offers concluding observations, 
major findings, as well as future research directions.

16
CHAPTER ONE – LITERATURE REVIEW I 
 
 
1. Introduction 
Research into financial system fraud detection maintains its vital status because fraudulent 
activities create massive economic losses and security vulnerabilities. Financial institutions use 
centralized supervision systems with rule-based systems for detecting unusual trades. Digital 
transformation in financial systems and their increasing complexity have not stopped fraudsters 
from inventing progressively sophisticated methods which bypass detection strategies. The 
decentralized financial landscape presents an extreme challenge for fraud detection because there 
is no centralized monitoring to help identify fraudulent activities. 
Fraud detection proves difficult on blockchain networks which include Bitcoin and Ethereum 
because they maintain decentralization together with pseudo-anonymity and unalterable features. 
Since blockchain transactions exist in an open environment they are publicly detectable yet 
financial institutions cannot enforce real-time monitoring or transaction management. The open 
nature of these systems creates spaces for criminals to use system vulnerabilities to perform 
money laundering and Ponzi schemes together with various other forms of financial scams. 
Advanced analytic techniques must be used to identify fraudulent activity inside blockchain 
networks because these networks present complex transaction patterns. 
Artificial intelligence (AI) teamed up with machine learning (ML) provides industry-leading 
offensive capabilities for fraud detection through automated analytical methods superior to 
conventional guideline-based systems. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) stand out among all 
these models for detecting complex network relationships in transactional patterns. Blockchain 
transactions maintain a natural graph structure through wallets represented by nodes and 
transactions connecting them by edges so GNNs serve as a powerful tool for identifying 
suspicious patterns while utilizing network topology. 
The research examines recent studies about fraud detection through blockchain-based systems 
while focusing on the application of GNNs. The research investigates blockchain fraud

17
difficulties and standard detection methods and demonstrates the beneficial aspects of graph-
based solutions. The research examines the relationship between GNNs and traditional detection 
methods while delivering extensive insight about their ability to safeguard financial networks 
that operate through decentralized mechanisms. 
2. Blockchain Transactions and Fraud Detection 
2.1 Blockchain Technology and Transaction Structure 
Financial ecosystems have experienced a transformation through blockchain technology which 
enables decentralized transactions that occur with transparent transparency while eliminating 
intermediary requirements. Singh (2024) conducts an extensive study about Decentralized 
Finance (DeFi) which shows how blockchain and cryptocurrencies act as fundamental 
components to transform conventional financial structures. The analysis points out that 
blockchain technology achieves peer-to-peer transactions through its implementation of smart 
contracts and decentralized ledger technology.  
 
Figure 1: DeFi Stack 
(Source: Singh, 2024)

18
Abdulhakeem and Hu (2021) analyze the impact of Bitcoin and Ethereum blockchain networks 
on financial systems by offering a different solution to conventional banking. The blockchain 
technology helps create financial inclusion for unbanked people because it allows for secure 
transactions that do not require permission. 
All blockchain transactions operate through a defined system based on wallet addresses that 
work as network nodes with each executed transaction establishing a directed edge between 
addresses. Enabling security analyses through blockchain transaction graphs serves as the core 
subject of Cholevas et al. (2024) research.  
 
Figure 2: Account and Transaction Presentation as Network 
(Source: Cholevas et al., 2024) 
Blockchain transactions possess graph-like characteristics which enable analysts to deploy 
sophisticated network-based methods according to the research of Cholevas et al. (2024). The 
secure blockchain system gives robust fraud detection capabilities because it stays changeless 
and shows all data but faces difficulties detecting fraudulent behaviors due to its pseudonymous 
design. 
2.2 Fraud in Blockchain Networks 
While blockchain adds security benefits, there are still fraudulent activities. In a blockchain 
network, Kutera (2022) identifies the major types of fraud, including money laundering, Ponzi 
schemes, and illegal transactions. It nonetheless points out that cryptocurrency money laundering 
is still the most common type of fraud, followed by financial pyramid schemes. Similar to this

19
Trozze et al. (2022), conducted a scoping review of cryptocurrency fraud which yields 47 unique 
fraud types that include pump and dump schemes, ransomware attacks, and high yield 
investment programs. According to their findings, the fraud in these decentralized networks is 
becoming more and more innovative and has its own methods as more and more people adopt 
cryptocurrencies. 
 
Figure 3: Co-occurrence analysis of the authors’ keywords 
(Source: Kutera., 2022) 
 
The problem of being unable to identify fraudulent transactions is one of the main challenges. 
Such banking systems are subject to regulation by regulatory bodies and financial institutions 
while blockchain transactions take place in a private and open environment so that it becomes 
comparatively more difficult to track suspicious activities (Singh, 2024). According to 
Abdulhakeem and Hu (2021), whilst decentralization is beneficial to financial inclusion, it at the 
same time provides opportunities for fraudsters to exploit. Still, they say that blockchain’s 
pseudonymity lends itself to malicious actors hiding their identity so that it’s hard to follow the 
trail of illicit transactions. Additionally, blockchain transactions unlike traditional financial 
systems are irreversible, hence it exposes you to risk of fraud.

20
Kutera (2022) compares blockchain fraud detection with traditional banking fraud prevention 
and concludes that financial institutions resort to using well known monitoring: transaction 
scoring and behavior analysis to identify fraudulent activities. In contrast, blockchain networks 
depend on the use of various detection techniques that make use of network structures, 
transaction history, and anomaly detection algorithms. As Trozze et al. (2022) suggest, new 
regulatory frameworks to combat cryptocurrency fraud and should implement measures to 
mitigate risks related to illicit financial activities. 
2.3 Existing Fraud Detection Techniques for Blockchain 
Traditional statistical methods, machine learning algorithms and graph-based methods are some 
of the existing fraud detection techniques in blockchain networks. Cholevas et al. (2024) provide 
a general survey of how to detect anomalies in blockchain using unsupervised learning 
techniques. Based on data integration strategies, they classify existing techniques into three main 
categories and explain that by far the most important special cases, in terms of understanding 
blockchain transactions, are unsupervised learning models, cluster analysis and density-based 
detection. 
In the area of traditional finance, as well as in blockchain networks, there are widely used rule-
based approaches, e.g. transaction monitoring systems, to detect fraud. Nevertheless, these 
methods are limited since they rely on the predefined thresholds and static rules (Kutera, 2022). 
In Ashfaq et al (2022), it is proposed that a machine learning based fraud detection mechanism is 
built upon blockchain and classification models. In order to detect fraudulent transactions in the 
Bitcoin network, they apply XGBoost and Random Forest algorithms. Using machine learning 
models, their findings show that not only do traditional rule-based techniques not perform well 
on learning anomalies and distributed behavioral characteristics, but that machine learning 
models are more accurate, automatically learning transaction patterns. 
It has also been widely used supervised and unsupervised learning techniques in blockchain 
fraud detection. Ashfaq et al. (2022) show that supervised models such as XGBoost and Random 
Forest are capable of classifying fraudulent transactions well if you have labeled training data. 
But they agree the challenge lies in acquiring high quality labeled data in the blockchain 
networks. As we do not need labeled datasets, Cholevas et al. (2024) recommend us to use

21
unsupervised learning approaches to maintain to detect novel fraud patterns. They further 
categorize the available unsupervised learning algorithms used in blockchain anomaly detection 
in the context of clustering, autoencoders, and graph-based techniques. 
3. Graph-Based Approaches in Fraud Detection 
3.1 Graph Theory and Network Representation of Blockchain Transactions 
The blockchain system creates an interconnected wallet network since wallet addresses operate 
as network nodes and transactions connect each address to other addresses through edges. The 
study by Wu et al. (2023) establishes that representing blockchain transaction records with 
graphs facilitates both transaction tracking operations and fraud detection processes. The authors 
present TRacer as a transaction tracing solution built upon graph theory which employs 
PageRank analysis to find relevant connections between wallets in order to uncover suspicious 
behavior. Through their research they show that blockchain transactions in decentralized finance 
(DeFi) are complex while demonstrating how graph-based search methods help identify fraud. 
Using network structures to analyze blockchain transactions enables users to gain insights about 
transaction movement patterns. Serena et al. (2022) established that cryptocurrency transaction 
networks manifest small-world features because almost every link between transactions exists 
within close proximity of each other. Their analysis shows that detection techniques of fraud 
should use graph structures to discover clusters of fraudulent activities. Transactions studied 
separately through conventional detection techniques offer reduced effectiveness compared to 
graph-based strategies because they understand financial relationships within a holistic 
framework. 
The detection system achieves superior results through graph-based analysis since it analyzes 
both standalone transactions and their relationships throughout the network. The efficiency of 
tracking illicit financial activities gets boosted through graph-based search algorithms according 
to Wu et al. (2023) which enables strong detection of money laundering schemes together with 
fraudulent accounts.

22
3.2 Applications of Graph Analytics in Fraud Detection 
The wide application of graph analytics in fraud detection utilizes techniques which include 
community detection and link prediction. Li et al. (2022) established that fraud classification 
systems become more effective when they join topological structures with transactional features 
using graph learning approaches. The TA-Struc2Vec model enables better financial fraud 
detection through its ability to track relationships inside financial networks. Research results 
show that graph-based analysis achieves superior performance compared to normal tabular 
transaction methods in machine learning models. 
Graph-based techniques have proven useful for detecting money laundering activities. According 
to Dumitrescu et al. (2022) bank defect detection using anomaly detection methods on 
transaction networks leads to better identification of financial irregularities. The research 
develops egonet structures and circular transaction flow indicators to boost the capacity of 
detecting anomalies within financial activities. Their framework successfully detects money 
laundering patterns through the combination of machine learning techniques with graph-based 
aspects. 
Graph anomaly detection serves blockchain fraud detection and additionally supports financial 
fraud investigations and security monitoring needs within cybersecurity. Studies by Ma et al. 
(2021) show that deep learning-based graph anomaly detection methods operate across domains 
that include fraud detection and network security together with social spam detection. Their 
research classifies contemporary detection methods according to which aspects of the network 
get identified including nodes, edges and subgraphs. Deep learning techniques provide 
exceptional performance at detecting fraudulent patterns which stay hidden from statistical 
methods that previously handled these tasks. 
The detection of fraud activities through graph-based methods finds widespread application 
within distributed ledger technologies (DLTs). The analysis of transaction networks in 
cryptocurrencies mentioned by Serena et al., 2022; shows specific behavioral patterns suitable 
for detecting fraud. Researchers used their investigation to prove graph-based detection methods 
search for nonstandard transaction groups and characteristic anomalies. Groups of researchers

23
led by Li et al. (2022) proved that graph learning models enhance the identification of fraud by 
recognizing intricate transaction patterns. 
4. Introduction to Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) 
4.1 Overview of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) 
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) represent an advanced system for handling graph-based 
information which enables deep learning models to extract knowledge from networks that link 
different entities. Traditional machine learning models with tabular data dependencies are not 
effective because GNNs can detect network relations between nodes and edges in order to find 
fraud in financial transactions. Khemani et al., 2024; explains that GNNs process information 
through message-passing operations which lets connected nodes exchange data so the model can 
draw insights from graph-based local and global structure patterns. GNNS have boosted the 
performance of node classification and link prediction and anomaly detection through their 
incorporation of relational data into deep learning structures. 
The main advantage of GNNs emerges from their capability to analyze intricate network 
systems. Deep learning methods based on convolutional and recurrent neural networks fail to 
process data with non-Euclidean formats which obstructs their application in graph relationship 
analysis. According to Prakash, 2024; GNNs enhance deep learning techniques by supporting 
graph-structured data and integrate node spatial relations which leads to better learning 
performance. Alongside Graph Convolutional Networks and Graph Attention Networks the 
research demonstrates how these architectural components use node feature aggregation and 
attention mechanisms for better graph representation learning. 
4.2 Types of GNN Models for Fraud Detection 
Various GNN architectures are developed to detect fraud in blockchain transactions by means of 
network relationships, and this choice is made for explicit functional reasons. In the past, graph 
convolutional networks (GCNs) have achieved wide spread utilization since they can aggregate 
information from neighboring nodes to model transaction patterns over blockchain networks. 
The reason GCNs use convolution on graphs (as opposed to matrices), as adopted from 
convolutional neural networks, is that they use convolutional operations on graphs according to

24
Khemani et al. (2024). Moreover, GCNs can boost anomaly detection by learning from the 
proximity and features of other nodes via learning neighborhood connectivity while in 
transaction graphs. 
Another model Graph Attention Networks (GATs) are another important model in which 
attention mechanism is used to differentiate the main and not important node connections. Since 
Wei and Lee (2024) assign different importance weights to transaction relations in GATs, the 
model has more chance to concentrate on high-risk relations. In their study, they bring forward a 
dual-channel graph attention network for financial fraud detection where attention-based 
approaches surpass the traditional fraud detection models. 
To avoid scalability issues, GraphSAGE presents another GNN architecture that introduces a 
method of aggregation for node neighborhoods that is both efficient and learning. Kadam (2024) 
argues that GraphSage can learn inductively on large scale transaction graphs by sampling and 
aggregation of information from a subset of neighboring nodes. It tremendously decreases the 
computational complexity while still preserving major caching transaction patterns, and it thus 
seems suitable for fighting against fraud in huge blockchain networks. 
4.3 Advantages of GNNs Over Traditional Machine Learning Methods 
The main advantage of GNNs over traditional fraud detection techniques lies in their capability 
to take into account contextual relations in blockchain transactions. However, traditional 
machine learning techniques like decision trees and support vector machines tend to rely on hand 
crafted features and are unable to incorporate complex network dependencies. Khosravi et al. 
(2025) argue that such limitation is overcome by the GNN based fraud detection models that 
learn directly from transaction graphs and hence can detect such subtle patterns related to 
fraudulent behavior. To improve fraud detection accuracy, this study introduces a spatial 
temporal GNN based on the transaction structure as well as temporal dependencies. 
Another important aspect made by GNN is the ability to comprehend interconnects patterns in 
transactions. Unlike rule-based methods, GNNs learns from evolving transaction networks in a 
dynamic fashion. The application of graph attention mechanisms in GNN models helps GNN 
models adapt against new fraudulent strategies and become more robust to adversarial attacks,

25
according to Wei and Lee (2024). Analysis of the research also shows that even when fraudsters 
try to conceal their actions by making them look like normal transactions, attention based GNNs 
can still recognize fraud activities. 
In addition, other benefits of GNNs for blockchain fraud detection include scalability and 
adaptability. Since blockchain networks generate large scale graphs, fraud detection models have 
to be efficient to process such graphs. As GNNs such as GraphSAGE and Graph Transfomers 
adopt efficient neighborhood sampling techniques, they are able to handle massive transaction 
networks without affecting performance, as mentioned by Prakash (2024). The study, however, 
argues that both these models can be used in the real time fraud detection systems for faster and 
more accurate detection of suspicious transactions. 
5. Comparative Analysis: GNNs vs. Traditional Fraud Detection Methods 
5.1 Traditional Machine Learning and Anomaly Detection Techniques 
Traditional machine learning techniques like logistic regression, decision trees and random 
forests have been the technique preferred for fraud detection because of their interpretability and 
efficiency. Both these models train on predefined features and classify transactions based on 
predefined features; however, these models use an example from the past for training. As stated 
by Zade (2024), random forest models generally perform very well in terms of fraud detection, 
and when hyperparameter tuning is done on the models. It also discovers that isolation forests are 
not optimal, as they rely on unsupervised learning, which is hard when the dataset is imbalanced. 
Transactions extremely deviated from normal patterns are identified using autoencoders or 
isolation forests for anomaly detection. Yoo et al. (2023) noted that isolation forests only perform 
poorly in the detection of fraud, especially in the case where fraudsters are continually changing 
their ways. It also suggests that graph centrality measures can be used to improve Medicare fraud 
detection recall rates when used in conjunction with the traditional machine learning models. But 
these systems are not as useful for detecting emerging patterns of fraud, for reasons involving 
ability to rely on predefined features within transactions.

26
5.2 Performance Comparison of GNNs with Traditional Models 
Current fraud detection models that involve relational structure between transactions have been 
greatly improved by the use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Ren et al. (2023) describe that 
GNN based methods are superior to traditional machine learning techniques on the detection of 
fraud as they are able to take into account dependencies between entities. To analyze the network 
structures, GNN models, such as Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN), Graph Attention 
Networks (GAT), and Graph Autoencoders (GAE), are categorized in the study and shown to be 
capable of discriminating fraudulent transactions. 
Discussions on the performance comparison between the traditional models and GNNs indicate 
that GNNs achieves higher precision, recall, and F1 scores. Zhang et al. (2022) utilized a 
competitive graph neural network model to predict fraudulent behaviors on the Taobao e-
commerce platform with eFraudCom. It shows that GNN based fraud detection can better adapt 
to new fraud patterns than traditional models, as they do not solely rely on historical fraud data. 
The system's capability to independently model both normal and fraudulent behavior enhances 
its ability to detect emerging fraud schemes more effectively. 
However, GNNs for fraud detection suffer from computational efficiency advantage. According 
to Yoo et al. (2023), training GNN is nearly 250–300 times more time consuming than that of the 
most effective traditional machine learning model. GNNs show good performance in fraud 
detection but are not scalable for real time applications. 
6. Research Gap 
Regardless, existing approaches still have significant limitations in regards to the fraud detection 
within blockchain networks. Existing traditional machine learning models (e.g. decision trees, 
random forests and autoencoders) require a priori predefined transaction variables without 
learning succeeding complex transaction interrelations (Kutera, 2022; Ashfaq et al., 2022). As 
unsupervised learning techniques try to address this issue, the evolution of fraudulent strategies 
makes them exhibit high false positive rates (Cholevas et al., 2024).

27
Studies have demonstrated the promise of graph-based fraud detection techniques in exploiting 
transaction dependencies (Wu et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022). However, most approaches utilize 
simple network analytics like clustering and centrality measures to detect fraud that cannot 
generalize to novel fraud patterns (Dumitrescu et al., 2022). In addition, graph anomaly detection 
studies (Ma et al., 2021) indicate that more advanced deep learning is needed to improve 
detection accuracy. 
GNNs similarly have shown promise when it comes to fraud detection, however, their use in 
blockchain transaction still remains less explored. As shown by Wei and Lee (2024), Prakash 
(2024), the comparison between GNNs and traditional methods in blockchain fraud detection has 
not been extensively researched. Therefore, this research attempts to fill this gap by employing 
GNNs to enhance the anomaly detection in decentralized financial networks. 
7. Summary 
All currently available blockchain fraud detection techniques are based on traditional machine 
learning, rule-based techniques as well graph-based analytics. Unfortunately, these approaches 
have not been effective in handling the detailed transaction dependencies. While graph-based 
schemes can help detect fraudulent schemes, they are still not adaptable to evolving fraudulent 
schemes. Learning transaction patterns directly from blockchain graphs by Graph Neural 
Networks (GNNs) is an appealing alternative. But they are still not used to detect fraud in 
blockchain. This research works to fill this gap by using GNNs based models to enhance fraud 
detection in the decentralized finance system, and compares to traditional anomaly detection 
techniques.

28
CHAPTER TWO – LITERATURE REVIEW II 
 
1. Introduction 
With the increased adoption of blockchain technology comes new challenges for fraud detection, 
specifically in decentralized finance (DeFi) networks. Given blockchain transactions are 
pseudonymous and lack centralized oversight, and having complex and dynamic relationships 
between entities, traditional fraud detection techniques such as rule base systems and 
conventional machine learning (ML) models are insufficient in identifying fraudulent activities 
within blockchain transactions. Unlike centralized financial systems the blockchain transactions 
are formed through intricate networks and so require more sophisticated forms of analytical 
techniques. 
As a solution to detect fraudulent patterns in blockchain transactions, Graph Neural Networks 
(GNNs) have emerged. Unlike traditional ML models, GNNs study the transactions in the 
blockchain transaction graph not as independent data points, but rather diaphanously tease out 
the structural referentiality in the graph itself for a deeper understanding of the complex of fraud 
behaviors. With the help of relational information, GNNs are able to detect anomalous patterns 
of transactions, located fake addresses, and discover the hidden connections of atrocious actors. 
With this structure GNN based fraud detection become more robust, scalable and adaptable to 
changing fraudulent behavior in decentralized networks. 
This chapter fully studies the GNN fraud detection techniques from the aspects of 
methodologies, advantages and limitations of other approaches. With this, the historical methods 
of fraud detection are explored and how they transitioned to graph-based approaches are 
discussed. The article then proceeds in providing an in-depth comparison between the alternative 
fraud detection methodologies and showing that GNNs outdo them all. The chapter also 
discusses datasets and evaluation metrics mostly used in the blockchain fraud detection research. 
In addition, it discusses problems of real time scalability, and legal and ethical aspects of AI 
based fraud detection in decentralized finance. The study is to provide a review of GNNs in 
order to showcase the importance that they bring to the table in the study of Blockchain fraud 
detection and to identify areas of future research.

29
2. Historical Background 
2.1 Traditional Fraud Detection Methods in Centralized Finance 
Fraud detection in conventional financial networks has evolved significantly over the years. 
Narayan, Shukla, and Kanth (2024) observe that early fraud detection relied heavily on rule-
based systems, where heuristic approaches and predefined thresholds were used in identifying 
suspicious activities. While these systems were sufficient for structured financial transactions, 
they were not sufficiently extensible to respond to changes in the ways that fraud evolves. The 
fraud detection systems incorporated machine learning (ML) models such as support vector 
machines (SVMs), decision trees, and logistic regression with the sophistication of fraudulent 
techniques. As stated by Luo et al. (2024), ML-based approaches led to better fraud detection 
from looking at transaction patterns but were inhibited by the fact that they required huge 
volumes of labeled data as well as their capability to learn complicated, hidden fraud patterns. 
However, while these advancements were made, traditional financial fraud detection based 
mostly on centralized oversight was responsible for regulatory compliance, and provided a way 
to ensure responsibility. But, for financial systems that were advancing towards decentralized 
finance (DeFi), these methods were unsatisfactory since there was no central intermediaries and 
such that blockchain transactions came with pseudonymity. 
2.2 Shift to Blockchain and Decentralized Finance (DeFi) 
Fraud detection changed its paradigm with the advent of blockchain based financial networks. As 
reported by Adisa et al. (2024), DeFi has grown since the blockchain transactions did not have 
traditional supervising mechanisms, which led to the increase of fraudulent activities that involve 
money laundering, Ponzi schemes, phishing and smart contract exploits. While blockchain 
enhances transparency with its immutable ledgers, authors argue that it also introduces 
significant security risks, as hackers can exploit anonymous transactions that are nearly 
impossible to trace. 
Makarov and Schoar (2022) also explain that one of the good things to DeFi is that it is 
pseudonymous and permissionless; fraud prevention in such protocols is challenging to do, 
especially since it is hard to perform AML controls and tax compliance. They explain how the 
lack of a central regulator allows malicious actors to operate across jurisdictions without

30
accountability. Aquilina, Frost, and Schrimpf (2024) add to this by explaining how DeFi 
applications, in attempting to disintermediate traditional financial services, also inherit the same 
risks, including market failures, information asymmetries, and consumer protection risks. These 
risks necessitate more advanced fraud detection systems that are custom-built for decentralized 
settings. 
2.3 Emergence of Graph-Based Fraud Detection 
To counter the challenges of DeFi fraud, researchers have more recently turned to graph-based 
fraud detection techniques. Narayan, Shukla, and Kanth (2024) discuss the potential of graph 
analytics and AI-driven techniques, in particular Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), for detecting 
fraudulent activity in DeFi. Unlike traditional ML models that treat transactions as independent 
data points, GNNs leverage transactional relationships in blockchain networks, which allows for 
pattern recognition and anomaly detection. 
Luo et al. (2024) also highlight the excellence of tree-based and graph-related models for fraud 
detection on various phases of DeFi projects. Their article develops a taxonomy of fraud and 
demonstrates how graph-based techniques can uncover hidden relationships among fraudulent 
entities. By structuring transactions into graph frameworks, these models have the capability to 
detect abnormal transaction flows, reveal suspicious clusters, and discover money-laundering 
patterns more precisely than conventional fraud detection methods. 
3. Comparison of Alternative Approaches 
3.1 Rule-Based Systems 
Most existing fraud detection systems employ the rule-based approaches in which predefined 
rules and threshold are used to detect fraud transactions. As noted by Niao et al. (2024), such 
methods have been widely used to underground their interpretability and simplicity to the 
centralized banking. However, they are limited in their ability to detect moving fraudulent assets 
and have high false positive rates. It also draws attention of Ahmed et al. (2021) to diminished 
efficiency of rule-based systems in detecting advanced, sophisticated fraud schemes which 
change over time.

31
While rule-based fraud detection systems had proven to be effective in the traditional banking, 
they were unsuccessful in DeFi. As blockchains lack central control and pseudonymity of 
transactions, there is no rule or rules that can capture adapting fraud techniques. So, there have 
been much more adaptive approaches in use to prevent fraud in decentralized financial networks, 
like machine learning and graph based. 
3.2 Machine Learning Approaches 
Machine learning (ML) has already significantly enhanced fraud detection by automating 
anomaly detection and pattern recognition. Niao et al. (2024) note that supervised learning 
models such as Random Forest, XGBoost, and Support Vector Machines (SVMs) have achieved 
high performance when trained on large, labeled datasets. Such models have the ability to 
identify known fraud patterns and mark transactions accordingly. The greatest limitation of 
supervised ML approaches in DeFi is the unavailability of labeled fraud data, which restricts 
their scalability and applicability in blockchain-based financial networks. 
To overcome these limitations, scientists have proposed unsupervised learning techniques, such 
as clustering algorithms, autoencoders, and isolation forests. Ahmed et al. (2021) propose a 
semantic rule-based fraud detection system, combining ontology-based reasoning and 
unsupervised learning for improving fraud prevention. Their approach demonstrates potential for 
detecting hitherto unknown fraud patterns, and therefore, it is apt for new fraud scheme 
detection. Unsupervised ML models, however, produce high false positive rates, as they are 
unable to adequately distinguish between legitimate and fraudulent transactions in evolving and 
dynamic financial ecosystems. 
3.3 Graph-Based Techniques 
Due to the nature of blockchain transactions being inherently interrelated, graph-based fraud 
detection techniques have been chosen to be a more viable solution. In the work of Souravlas, 
Anastasiadou and Katsavounis [2021], they explore the use of community detection techniques 
for fraud detection, where the fraudsters form densely connected, clusters in technology 
transaction graphs. Their work showcases the ability of graph clustering methods, including 
centrality based as well as modularity based, to detect anomalous transaction patterns. However,

32
often these techniques are based on the usage of static graph structures, so they can't keep up 
with emerging, dynamic, and evolving fraud tactics.  
3.4 Graph Machine Learning Approaches 
Recent developments in graph-based machine learning have encouraged the development of 
more adaptive and scalable fraud detection models. Zita (2024) introduces a Graph Autoencoder 
(GAE) approach to community detection, demonstrating how autoencoders learn complex 
transaction patterns in blockchain networks. They categorize GAEs into simple encoder and dual 
encoder models, demonstrating that dual encoder models with attention mechanisms perform 
better than traditional clustering approaches in identifying fraud-related communities. 
Similarly, de Sapienza Luna (2023) proposes a Graph Neural Network (GNN) approach to fraud 
detection in node-attributed networks. Their research focuses on the superiority of GNNs in 
representing multi-hop transaction dependencies, thus allowing for more accurate fraud detection 
in decentralized networks. Unlike traditional ML models, GNNs possess the ability to learn 
directly from the structural characteristics of blockchain transaction graphs, and therefore, they 
are highly effective in identifying sophisticated fraud patterns, such as money laundering and 
transaction obfuscation. 
3.5 Why GNNs Are Superior 
GNNs serve as the current peak in fraud detection techniques for decentralized finance. GNNs 
deliver advantages above rule-based and traditional ML systems through their capabilities: 
GNNs enable direct learning from Blockchain graphs by processing the blockchain graph 
structure natively without needing human-manual data labeling. The majority of fraudulent 
operations require transactions involving multiple account stops since crime funds flow through 
several intermediary wallets. GNNs demonstrate exceptional capability in detecting hidden 
money laundering trails across the blockchain therefore remaining highly effective for fraud 
detection. GNNs exhibit an ability to adapt their fraud pattern detection capabilities because they 
automatically develop new skills as emerging fraud strategies evolve in time. The technology 
suits DeFi applications because attackers persistently create new methods to hide their activities 
from detection systems.

33
4. Specific Methodologies in GNN-Based Fraud Detection 
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become a promising solution for fraud detection in 
blockchain networks by facilitating the detection of fraudulent behavior through the leveraging 
of the relational structure of transaction networks. Various studies have examined various GNN 
architectures and strategies for improving the performance, scalability, and flexibility of fraud 
detection in blockchain environments. The following is an overview of the key approaches in 
GNN-based fraud detection, as reported by Hasebe (2024), Ancelotti and Liason (2024), Maturi 
and Stockton (2024), and other researchers. 
4.1 Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for Blockchain Fraud Detection 
GNNs have been widely applied to represent blockchain transaction networks, where nodes are 
user addresses and edges are transactions. Using the exploitation of the graph structure in the 
blockchain data, GNNs enable the fraud detection models to learn suspicious pattern, multi-hop 
fraud scams, and hidden relationships between different malicious actors. In addition, the 
following key GNN architectures have been explored as a part of blockchain fraud detection. 
A wide variety of application to the structural features for blockchain transaction graph has been 
provided by Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs). In Maturi and Stockton (2024), GCNs are 
used to transform Ethereum transaction data to graph form to find fraudulent transactions using 
node embeddings and relational features. Indeed, in their findings, they show that the GCN-
based fraud detection model has high accuracy, precision, and recall, which means that graph 
convolution is an excellent tool to learn about transaction patterns. 
Likewise, Ancelotti and Liason (2024) have used GCNs to create blockchain node and 
transaction models and illustrate how hierarchical feature extraction from transaction networks is 
advantageous for those models using GCNs. However, with large blockchains, GCNs are limited 
by their computationally expensive convolutional operations. 
This makes GATs better than GCNs in the sense that GATs have different attention weights 
assigned to the adjacent nodes, therefore the model can emphasize more on the relevant 
relationships in a transaction graph. As Hasebe (2024) mentions, the application of GATs for 
detecting fraudulent cryptocurrency transactions based on learning contextual dependencies is 
effective for detecting high risk transactions in general. Dynamically changing attention weights

34
allows the model to focus on suspicious wallet interactions, improving fraud detection accuracy 
through GATs. 
This work is motivated that one of the most critical scalability limitations of GCNs is to seek an 
alternative learning paradigm for node embeddings of large scale blockchain networks, namely 
Graph Sample and Aggregation (GraphSAGE). According to Hasebe (2024), graph convolving 
all the neighbors given a node can accomplish the job efficiently by sampling only a few 
neighbors. This method offers the reduction of computational burden without degrading the 
detection performance, which is suitable for fraud detection on high-transaction blockchain 
network in real time. 
Temporal dependencies should be captured in the fraud detection models in blockchain networks 
where frauds evolve over time. In blockchain fraud detection, Chen et al. (2025) propose using 
Multi-Distance Spatial-Temperature Graph Neural Networks (MDST-GNNs) to learn temporal 
graphs with the intention to better detect anomalies in the crypto transaction process. They 
integrate adaptive temporal modeling with time-based graph convolution by capturing short term 
and long-term fraud patterns. Experimental analysis shows that temporal graph models, as 
opposed to the static GNNs, produces significantly better results in real time fraud detection. 
4.2 Key Techniques Used in GNN-Based Fraud Detection 
Apart from architectural innovation, GNN-based fraud detection employs different techniques to 
classify transactions, predict fraudulent relationships, and generate graph embeddings for 
anomaly detection. 
One of the well-known tasks in fraud detection is classifying blockchain addresses (nodes) as 
fraudulent or honest. Ganguly (2023) describes the issue of class imbalance in fraud detection, 
where fraudulent transactions are a minority of the dataset. To improve classification 
performance, their study employs multi-task learning GNNs, which combine node classification 
with auxiliary tasks (such as link prediction and graph embeddings) to render the model more 
robust. Their findings show that multi-task learning significantly improves fraud classification 
performance by taking advantage of additional relational signals in blockchain networks. 
Similarly, Maturi and Stockton (2024) point out the role of GCN-based node classification in 
blockchain fraud detection, demonstrating how node features can be strengthened through

35
preprocessing, feature engineering, and embedding techniques. They demonstrate through their 
experiments that GNNs consistently perform better than traditional machine learning models in 
the classification of fraudulent blockchain addresses. 
Fraudulent activities have a tendency to follow some patterns, where illegal wallets interact with 
multiple compromised accounts for money laundering. Liu, Chen, and Wen (2023) discuss the 
application of link prediction in detecting suspicious transactional relationships using GNN-
based embeddings for inferring hidden connections between fraudulent parties. The review 
discusses how graph classification and link prediction algorithms help uncover multi-hop fraud 
rings for improving detection efficiency in decentralized finance (DeFi). 
Chen et al. (2025) also explore self-supervised learning for link prediction, demonstrating multi-
distance graph models learning anomalous transaction links using limited labeled fraud data. 
Their results suggest self-supervised graph learning approaches can significantly enhance 
blockchain fraud detection by automatically discovering illegal transactional patterns.

Graph embedding techniques transform blockchain transactions into low-dimensional vector 
representations, making it possible to apply machine learning models for fraud detection. Kim et 
al. (2022) provide a comprehensive survey of graph anomaly detection techniques using GNNs, 
explaining how graph embeddings based on autoencoders can learn hidden fraud patterns in

36
transaction graphs. Their article categorizes graph embedding techniques based on network 
architecture, anomaly, and data representation, showing their application in identifying unusual 
transaction flows. 
Ganguly (2023) also examines feature enhancement through graph embeddings, combining link 
prediction and node classification to develop more sophisticated fraud detection models. Their 
approach highlights the merit in the combination of multiple graph-based tasks to improve the 
efficacy and robustness of blockchain fraud detection systems. 
5. Real-Time Scalability of GNNs in Blockchain 
Real-time scalability of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for blockchain use cases has been an 
open topic of research due to the prohibitive computational needs for training and deploying 
GNNs over large transaction graphs. Several works have investigated the challenges and 
solutions for scaling up GNN-based fraud detection models, particularly in dynamic and 
decentralized environments. 
5.1 Challenges in Real-Time GNN Deployment 
Applying GNNs to real-time blockchain fraud detection encounters significant computational 
challenges. Badr (2024) identifies scalability issues in traditional trust assessment models, 
particularly in the inclusion of GNNs for reputation systems. The GBTrust model improves fraud 
detection accuracy at the expense of computational complexity for large-scale applications. 
The vast size of blockchain networks also makes scalability challenging. Liang (2024) explains 
full-graph training inefficiencies and proposes distributed approaches like DistDGL and 
ByteGNN for reducing computational overhead. Bajaj et al. (2024) demonstrate that mini-batch 
training provides quicker convergence with no accuracy loss and, hence, is crucial for fraud 
detection in real time on blockchain networks. 
5.2 Optimizing GNNs for Scalability 
Several approaches have been proposed to scale up GNNs to blockchain fraud detection so that 
they can be efficiently executed on large transaction graphs. One of them is a sampling-based 
approach known as GraphSAGE, where GNNs can learn from a subset of neighbor nodes rather

37
than the entire graph. Badr (2024) stresses its efficiency in decentralized trust management with 
a focus on GBTrust, which selects trustworthy peers by random walks. 
Mini-batch training reduces memory needs without a loss in accuracy. Bajaj et al. (2024) show 
that mini-batch systems converge more quickly than full-graph training and are therefore 
amenable to real-time blockchain analytics. Md et al. (2021) introduce DistGNN, a scalable 
system that employs mini-batch sampling and graph partitioning. 
Distributed GNN architectures also improve efficiency. Liang (2024) presents Sancus, a 
decentralized GNN system with little data movement. Md et al. (2021) present a graph 
partitioning algorithm with up to 97× speed-up, which makes distributed GNNs possible for 
blockchain fraud detection. 
6. Research Gap 
The current techniques for blockchain fraud detection show progress but they encounter 
substantial difficulties. Decision trees and autoencoders among traditional machine learning 
models need predefined transaction variables which restricts their ability to detect new fraud 
patterns (Kutera, 2022; Ashfaq et al., 2022). Unsupervised learning methods try to overcome 
these limitations but they produce excessive false positives because of the changing nature of 
fraudulent activities (Cholevas et al., 2024). 
Graph-based fraud detection techniques demonstrate effective capabilities for analyzing 
transaction dependencies according to Wu et al. (2023) and Li et al. (2022). Static network 
analytical methods such as clustering and centrality measures used in many approaches fail to 
identify new emerging fraud patterns according to Dumitrescu et al. (2022). Deep learning 
algorithms at an advanced level must be implemented to boost blockchain network fraud 
detection precision (Ma et al., 2021). 
The implementation of GNNs as an adaptive fraud detection solution for blockchain networks 
requires further investigation. Research on GNN-based models in decentralized finance remains 
limited with minimal comparisons to traditional fraud detection techniques according to Wei and 
Lee (2024) and Prakash (2024). The research investigates how GNNs can detect blockchain 
fraud while enhancing anomaly detection precision and resolving scalability issues in real-time 
systems.

38
7. Summary 
Blockchain fraud detection transition from traditional machine learning techniques to graph-
based approaches is discussed in the literature review. Rule based or machine learning based 
fraud detection systems were early but lacked scalability; in finding fraud, they had issues 
evolving with fraud patterns and high false positive rates. 
Introducing graph-based techniques became more effective of detecting fraud by leveraging 
transaction dependencies. Some studies indicate that graph anomaly detection methods (e.g., 
clustering and centrality measures) can improve fraud detection accuracy compared to direct link 
addition methods (Wu et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022). However, they have a limited adaptability to 
changing fraudulent schemes. The more advanced solution offered by GNNs involves learning 
complex transaction relationships and multi-hop fraudulent activities. Fraud detection accuracy 
using techniques of Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) and Graph Attention Networks 
(GAT) are shown. Finally, methods that include GraphSAGE, mini batch training and distributed 
GNN architectures take care of scalability challenges (Badr, 2024; Liang, 2024; Bajaj et al., 
2024). This research adds to previous research by utilizing GNN based models for blockchain 
fraud detection against classical models and increasing the scalability for real time detection.
