CHAPTER THREE – METHODOLOGY 
 
 
1. Introduction to the Methodology 
The chapter outlines the research methodology for studying blockchain-based fraud detection 
using Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). It details research design, tools and data resources, and 
analytical strategies to reach the study goals; developing an Artificial Intelligence system that 
uses blockchain network structure to identify fraudulent transactions effectively. 
Traditional machine learning methods fail to properly understand the complete transactional 
behavior patterns because blockchain networks operate with complex and interconnected 
transaction structures. This study models wallet addresses as nodes and transactions as edges in a 
graph-based approach using GCN, GAT and GraphSAGE as GNNs. 
The research methodology aims to create a transparent, scalable system for detecting 
decentralized financial anomalies as compared to traditional methods for practical blockchain 
applications.  
2. Research Design 
The study uses a quantitative approach to research due to the numerical standardization of 
blockchain transaction information according to Taherdoost (2022). The main purpose is to 
recognize fraudulent practices by using quantifiable patterns through essential statistical and 
computational models. The development and evaluation of fraud detection systems require 
objective analyses through mathematical models and performance metrics with statistical 
validation because of quantitative methods. 
The selection of a quantitative research design emerges from the data structure of Bitcoin 
transaction records which contain quantitative features consisting of transaction volume 
alongside fees and input-output counts as well as behavioral aggregation measures. The data 
features are well-suited for statistical inference models when combined with pattern recognition 
approaches.

40
The research design using experimental structures to analyze comparative data for predictive 
modeling. The experimental part relies on training and evaluating three Graph Neural Networks 
including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN), Graph Attention Networks (GAT), and 
GraphSAGE through labeled blockchain data (Firdaus, and Sumardi, 2024). The evaluation 
compares GNN model results to established machine learning methods which include Isolation 
Forest along with Autoencoders and Logistic Regression. This part of the design aims prediction 
since models analyze learned data patterns to determine if a transaction stands as legitimate or 
fraudulent. 
The research design provides methodological consistency and allows for a precise evaluation of 
deep learning techniques for blockchain fraud discovery. 
3. Research Method 
The research uses machine learning principles through graph-based modeling to identify illegal 
activities in blockchain networks. This system takes blockchain information and converts it into 
graph structures before implementing Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for finding abnormal 
patterns that signal deceptive behaviors. This research compares the effectiveness and 
performance of the approached machine learning technique with traditional methods to detect 
blockchain fraud. 
3.1 Machine Learning and Graph-Based Modeling 
The research foundation relies on the understanding that blockchain transaction data shows 
relational connections which need a graph-based representation. The network structure contains 
wallet addresses or transactions as nodes while asset transfers create connecting edges between 
the nodes. The relational structure of the blockchain data surpasses basic flat design 
representations because such structures fail to capture the intricate entity relationships. 
Graph-based modeling developed an improved approach to represent blockchain data through 
contextual bound topological understanding. Graph structures capture transaction individual 
properties as well as transaction relationships to understand fraudulent behavior through both 
explicit and implicit associations between entities. The financial fraud method that uses 
sophisticated transaction networks to hide origins and final destinations matches the typical 
patterns found in money laundering and Ponzi schemes.

41
Use of Graph Neural Networks (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) 
To fully leverage the structure of blockchain data, this study applies three types of Graph Neural 
Networks: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN), Graph Attention Networks (GAT), and 
GraphSAGE (Ponzi, and Napoli, 2025). 
Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN) apply traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) 
for processing graph data. GCNs combine local features from neighboring nodes to update the 
representation of individual nodes based on these gathered neighboring features (Bhatti, et al., 
2023). The use of GCNs in fraud detection allows machines to create transaction embeddings 
which integrate the individual node features and collective actions of connected nodes. 
With Graph Attention Networks (GAT) nodes learn to determine which neighboring network 
items require what attention weights during aggregation operations. In blockchain graphs the 
informative connections differ from each other so this concept becomes essential (Vrahatis, 
Lazaros, and Kotsiantis, 2024). The learned attention coefficients enable GAT models to 
concentrate analysis on important neighbors which improves the discovery of elusive fraudulent 
activities. 
The GraphSAGE system provides a mechanism which allows scalability of GNNs when working 
with large graphs. The method obtains a consistent number of neighboring nodes and applies 
function learning models that allow inductive analysis (Ma, Rong, and Huang, 2022). The 
algorithm suits blockchain networks well because of their extensive growth of new nodes and 
transactions by enabling real-time processing of added information. 
This study implements separate architectures to detect financial fraud because each model 
contributes different advantages to the domain analysis process. 
 
Comparison with Traditional Machine Learning Techniques 
To benchmark the performance of GNN-based methods, this research also implements three 
well-established machine learning algorithms: Isolation Forest, Autoencoders, and Logistic 
Regression.

42
Isolation Forest is an anomaly detection algorithm that isolates observations by randomly 
selecting features and split values. It is particularly effective for high-dimensional datasets and is 
often used in fraud detection due to its unsupervised nature. However, it does not utilize graph 
structure and thus may miss contextual anomalies. 
Autoencoders are neural networks trained to reconstruct their input. During training, the model 
learns to compress and decompress the data, and poor reconstruction performance may indicate 
an anomaly. Autoencoders are sensitive to subtle feature deviations but, like Isolation Forests, 
they do not account for relational data. 
Logistic Regression serves as a baseline for supervised classification. It is a simple, interpretable 
model that provides a point of comparison for evaluating the performance gains achieved by 
more complex architectures. Logistic Regression, however, treats data as independent instances 
and cannot model the dependencies inherent in transaction graphs. 
By including these methods, the research offers a holistic view of how graph-aware approaches 
compare with traditional models in terms of precision, recall, F1-score, and overall fraud 
detection accuracy. 
3.2 Explanation of Model Training and Evaluation 
Model training begins with data preprocessing, including the transformation of blockchain 
transactions into graph structures. This is achieved using the edgelist file from the Elliptic 
dataset, which defines the relationships between transactions. Features associated with each node 
are derived from the transaction metadata and are normalized to ensure consistency. 
For GNN models, the graph is constructed using the PyTorch Geometric library. Each model 
(GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) is trained using labeled data—nodes identified as either licit or illicit 
(Song, et al., 2021). A portion of the dataset is set aside for validation and testing to evaluate 
generalization performance. 
Hyperparameters such as learning rate, number of layers, hidden units, and dropout rates are 
optimized through grid search and validation accuracy. The models are trained using cross-
entropy loss for classification tasks, and Adam optimizer is used to accelerate convergence. 
Evaluation is conducted using multiple performance metrics:

43
Precision assesses the proportion of detected fraudulent transactions that are truly fraudulent. 
Recall evaluates the model's ability to find all relevant fraudulent transactions. 
F1-score balances precision and recall, especially important for imbalanced datasets. 
Confusion matrices are used to visualize classification performance across classes. 
Statistical tests such as t-tests are applied to determine if performance differences between 
models are significant. 
For traditional models, similar training and evaluation processes are followed, but without graph 
structural input. Feature matrices are flattened, and models are fit using scikit-learn 
implementations. 
Through this methodical approach, the study rigorously compares GNNs and traditional 
methods, identifying the strengths and limitations of each. The research demonstrates that 
incorporating graph structures provides a significant advantage in fraud detection, particularly in 
decentralized networks where relational context is key to understanding transaction behavior. 
4. Reason for Selecting This Methodology 
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) represent the main methodology choice because they suit the 
distinct structural characteristics and data patterns in blockchain systems. Blockchain transaction 
data creates extensive networks that connect every entry through wallet links and financial 
transactions. The relational nature of the GNN data structure makes the network a powerful 
modeling tool for analytics because GNNs specialize in learning from graph information. 
The traditional machine learning approach analyzes transactions in isolation from their network-
based contextual relationships. The capability for capturing fraudulent behavior which depends 
on advanced interconnected patterns remains restricted by these methods. GNNs enable the 
collection of information from neighboring nodes in addition to discovering both local and 
global patterns that exist in the transaction graph (Zhang, et al., 2024). Such contextual modeling 
methods serve as a basic requirement to discover money laundering operations and elaborate 
fraud schemes that emerge through collaboration between multiple participants.

44
The research makes an original contribution by deploying GNN architectures including GCN, 
GAT, and GraphSAGE for fraud detection on real-world blockchain data according to Vrahatis, 
Lazaros, and Kotsiantis (2024). The use of GNNs for decentralized financial systems stands as a 
relatively new investigation area with minimal existing research. The author addresses this 
scholarly void by showing how graph learning algorithms boost action detection throughout the 
study. 
Blockchain introduces real-time operating characteristics that need adaptable models to handle 
dynamic unstructured environments because of its decentralized design. GNNs offer the 
necessary flexibility together with scalability needed for these applications which makes them a 
suitable decision for this study's research. 
5. Research Instruments 
The research implements, trains and evaluates the fraud detection models using a collection of 
computation tools and open-source libraries in the Python ecosystem. 
PyTorch and PyTorch Geometric are the core libraries used. Pytorch gives a course supportive 
and productive stage for creating profound learning models, and Pytorch Grounded expands this 
surpassability to help graph-based learning. Building and training GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE 
over a verified graph structure is an essential part of this study and these tools are used for them 
(Firdaus, and Sumardi, 2024). Further, graph structures from the raw transactional data are built 
and manipulated by means of NetworkX and then converted to feed into a GNN model. 
The Elliptic Bitcoin Transaction Dataset on Kaggle provides real-world Bitcoin transaction data 
with 166 features per transaction, labelled as licit or illicit 
Matplotlib and Seaborn are used for the data visualizations to plot transaction patterns, model 
performance metric, and confusion matrix.  
LabelEncoder processes categorical labels for further processing and evaluation depending on 
data preprocessing and scikit-learn (sklearn) metrics for precision, recall, or F1 score; and torch 
functions to carry out training and optimization of the model.

45
6. Dataset and Sampling 
6.1 Dataset Description 
This study utilizes the Elliptic Bitcoin Transaction Dataset, a publicly available dataset hosted on 
Kaggle and developed by Elliptic, a blockchain analytics firm. It contains 203,769 nodes 
(transactions) and 234,355 edges (transaction flows). Each node has 166 numerical features, such 
as transaction volume and fees. Nodes are labeled licit (21%), illicit (2%), or unknown, 
supporting supervised classification. 
6.2 Sampling Techniques 
A non-probabilistic sampling approach is adopted, focusing exclusively on labelled (licit/illicit) 
transactions due to the high number of unlabelled nodes. The dataset’s 49-time steps, each 
spanning two weeks, provides chronological segmentation for temporal analysis. 
6.3 Sample Size 
The study uses the entire set of labeled transactions for model training and evaluation, totaling 
4,545 illicit and 42,019 licit samples. Due to the class imbalance, the dataset may be balanced 
through under-sampling of the majority class or the application of synthetic techniques like 
SMOTE to ensure fair model evaluation. This sampling strategy ensures that the supervised 
learning models are trained on reliable, labeled data that reflects realistic blockchain activity. 
7. Data Collection 
Primary data was collected using google forms questionnaire with 10 multiple choice and scaled 
questions to assess 50 respondents’: 
 Experience with cryptocurrency 
 Familiarity with blockchain 
 Exposure to fraud 
 Confidence in fraud detection 
 Trust in DeFi

46
 Awareness of blockchain graph structures 
 Perception of AI models like Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) 
The form was distributed online, and 50 valid responses were collected. The responses were 
visualized using Google Form’s built-in chart features, and analyzed qualitatively to test three 
specific hypotheses relevant to blockchain fraud awareness and detection. 
This primary dataset supports the broader dissertation, which involves secondary data analysis 
using the Elliptic Bitcoin Transaction Dataset and the application of Graph Neural Networks for 
fraud detection. 
 
For this study, the data used was taken from secondary sources, i.e., the Elliptic Bitcoin 
Transaction Dataset hosted on Kaggle. Since the dataset was available and precompiled and there 
has been no need to collect primary data, there was no need to involve human participants, as 
such there were no direct consent or ethical clearance involved for human subject research. 
The data were prepared for machine learning and graph-based modeling by doing a few 
preprocessing steps. To ensure uniformity in input distributions so that the range of all the 166 
numerical features is normalized or standardized to standard range first. Transactions classified 
as “unknown” were next removed from the dataset and only licit and illicit transactions were left 
for supervised learning. 
The preprocessing part was critical such that graph construction of relationships between 
transactions is achieved from edgelist file. And since it was a edgelist, it was converted into a 
graph object so as to utilize Graph Neural Networks. Thus, lastly, class labels were encoded as 
numerical values so that binary classification can be made, that is, licit transactions are assigned 
0 and illicit transactions are assigned 1. It ensured that the data was clean, well structured ready 
to be used for training and evaluating the model. 
8. Data Analysis Techniques 
The data analysis of this research was to build, train and evaluate several machine learning 
models that would classify blockchain transactions as either licit or illicit. Graph construction

47
kicked it off by taking the raw transactional data and converting it into a network representation. 
Using NetworkX, a graph was defined with each node representing one transaction and an edge 
signifying a connection between nodes (transactions) linked by Bitcoin flow. After this graph we 
then apply it with PyTorch Geometric which is specialized in designing tools for being able to 
apply Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on structured graph data. 
The central analytical task was node classification, where the goal was to label transactions 
based on their legitimacy. Three GNN architectures were used: 
Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN), which aggregate information from neighboring nodes to 
update the representation of each node. 
Graph Attention Networks (GAT), which enhance the aggregation process by assigning different 
importance to different neighbors using an attention mechanism (Khemani, et al., 2024). 
GraphSAGE, which supports inductive learning by sampling and aggregating information from a 
fixed number of neighboring nodes, enabling scalability to large graphs. 
To establish a performance benchmark, the research also implemented traditional machine 
learning models for comparison: 
Logistic Regression, a basic but interpretable linear classifier used on flat features. 
Isolation Forest, an unsupervised method for detecting outliers, well-suited for anomaly 
detection. 
Autoencoders, which learn to reconstruct their input and identify anomalies based on 
reconstruction error. 
These models were evaluated using several well-established performance metrics: 
Accuracy measured the proportion of correct predictions. 
Precision quantified how many of the transactions labeled as illicit were actually fraudulent. 
Recall indicated how many of the total fraudulent transactions were correctly identified. 
F1-score provided a harmonic mean of precision and recall, offering a balanced evaluation.

48
A confusion matrix was used to visualize the breakdown of predictions into true positives, true 
negatives, false positives, and false negatives. 
9. Ethical Considerations 
Ethical clearance was unnecessary as no human subjects were involved. The study exclusively 
utilizes the Elliptic Bitcoin Transaction Dataset, which is fully anonymized and made available 
on Kaggle for academic and research purposes. The study supports ethical goals by improving 
fraud detection in decentralized finance whilst adhering to responsible research practices and 
open-source policies. 
10. Summary of Methodology 
This chapter represents a methodological framework for blockchain fraud detection using Graph 
Neural Networks (GNNs). The study was conducted with a quantitative research design using 
structured transaction data. The key tools used were Python, PyTorch, PyTorch Geometric, 
NetworkX, Seaborn, Matplotlib, and scikit-learn.  
These tools were used for the node classification, detecting illicit operations from transaction 
graph layout and feature sets. Besides utilizing secondary data, the research involved integrating 
primary data through a guided questionnaire filled out by 50 respondents. The survey captured 
user experience with blockchain, fraud exposure, and attitudes toward AI models, 
complementing the study's fraud awareness and detection hypotheses. Comparative analysis 
among conventional models; Logistic Regression, Isolation Forest, and Autoencoders 
determined performance baselines compared to GNN models. Overall, methodology adequately 
met the challenge of detecting intricate illegal behavior in decentralized finance systems. By 
representing transactions as a graph and utilizing the relational learning functionality of GNNs, 
the work introduced an effective and scalable methodology for improving blockchain fraud 
detection and advancing financial cybersecurity as a field.

4.2 ANALYSIS 
The analysis section interprets and contextualizes the findings from both primary and secondary 
datasets, assessing the significance of the results in alignment with the research objectives and 
hypotheses. The evaluation reflects the core aim of the study: to determine the effectiveness of 
Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for fraud detection in blockchain systems and to compare their 
performance against traditional machine learning techniques. 
4.2.1 Analysis of Primary Dataset Findings 
The primary dataset, gathered through a structured Google Form survey, offers insight into user 
perceptions and understanding of blockchain technology, specifically relating to fraud detection 
mechanisms. The three hypotheses posed in the study were directly tested through responses, and 
each provided varying degrees of empirical support. 
For Hypothesis 1—whether users with greater familiarity with blockchain are more likely to trust 
decentralized finance (DeFi)—the findings indicated a general trend of positive correlation. A 
majority of respondents who rated their blockchain familiarity at the highest level also expressed 
trust in DeFi platforms. However, the presence of respondents who were highly familiar with 
blockchain yet distrustful of DeFi introduces complexity. This suggests that while technical 
knowledge can increase trust, it is not the sole determinant; personal experiences, especially 
involving fraud, likely influence trust levels. The small sample size (N=20) restricts the 
statistical generalizability of this observation but provides directional insights. 
Hypothesis 2 proposed that individuals who have encountered fraudulent transactions in the past 
are less confident in identifying future fraud. Interestingly, the data yielded a mixed outcome. 
While some participants with prior exposure to fraud exhibited lower confidence levels, others 
retained or even expressed high confidence. This divergence could be explained by differing 
psychological responses: some users become more cautious, while others develop a sense of 
expertise after repeated exposure. The result only partially supports the hypothesis and 
underlines the multifaceted nature of user behavior in financial systems.

66
Hypothesis 3 examined whether awareness of blockchain's graph structure is associated with 
greater belief in the effectiveness of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). This hypothesis was the 
most strongly supported of the three. Participants aware of the graph-based nature of blockchain 
transactions were more likely to affirm the utility of GNNs in fraud detection. This suggests that 
technical awareness fosters a more accurate perception of advanced detection methodologies and 
supports the relevance of using GNNs in blockchain security research. 
4.2.2 Analysis of Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) 
The secondary dataset, Elliptic Bitcoin Transactions, contained a rich set of features and 
structural relations to facilitate deeper exploration. The EDA shed light on a number of key areas 
influencing model development and interpretation. 
The class distribution chart showed a considerable skewing, with legitimate transactions 
accounting for more than 91% of the tagged data. The skewing presents a serious problem to 
predictive modeling, especially when attempting to identify infrequent illicit activity. Unless it is 
corrected, skewing will cause models to favor predicting the majority class. The choice to do 
class weighting when training the models was an essential countermeasure to guarantee equal 
learning and to increase sensitivity to the minority class (i.e., fraudulent activity). 
The first ten feature correlation heatmap presented evidence of multicollinearity in some features 
like feature_5 and feature_7 (∼0.99). Although some correlation is unavoidable, strongly 
correlated features indicate redundancy and could hamper model performance or even cause 
overfitting. Dimension reduction or removal of strongly related variables are essential feature 
engineering procedures to consider in subsequent model refinement. 
The bar graph of the top 10 features most associated with the binary class label also supported 
the multi-dimensionality of the data. Although features like feature_54 and feature_90 had 
indicative correlations, none of the features were predominantly high. This supported the use of 
deep learning models having the ability to combine multiple features and learn higher-order 
representations from the input data. 
The graph structure overview ensured the dataset is conducive to GNN modeling. The dataset 
contains more than 46,000 nodes and 36,000 edges and represents the networked and relational 
richness of environments found in blockchains. The number of node features (166) also provides

67
adequate dimensionality to learn from and the two-class system is amenable to a binary 
classification problem. 
4.2.3 Analysis of Graph Neural Network Models 
In the three implemented GNN models—GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE—the performance trend 
aligned with theoretical expectations drawn from the literature. Strong classification scores were 
seen by all models but with different precision, recall, and F1-score values. 
The GCN model did a good job, particularly in recall for illicit transaction (0.9098), which 
implies its efficiency in detecting fraudulent activities. Its relatively low precision (0.5325) for 
the same class shows a relatively high rate of false positives. This is a trade-off; even though the 
model is good at detecting most frauds, it tags a lot of honest ones as frauds too. In financial 
scenarios, false positives can erode user confidence and efficiency of processes. 
GAT outperformed GCN significantly by balancing recall and precision better. The attention 
mechanism enabled it to assign higher importance to more vital node connections, and as a 
result, it had a better overall accuracy (92.14%) and F1-score (0.6950) on illicit transactions. 
This indicates that incorporating interpretability and relational context through attention layers 
improves fraud detection performance, most notably in dense networks such as blockchain. 
GraphSAGE also outperformed both of its predecessors on virtually all the metrics. Ending at a 
record accuracy of 95.08% and a maximum F1-score of 0.7852 on the illicit class, it was 
extremely effective. Both the inductive learning methodology and neighborhood sampling 
mechanisms facilitated by it enabled it to scale successfully and identify fine-grained patterns on 
the network. The confusion matrix of GraphSAGE contained fewer examples of false positives 
and negatives, making it all the more feasible to apply in real-life fraud prediction contexts. 
4.2.4 Analysis of Traditional Machine Learning Models 
Conventional machine learning models had relatively poorer performance, confirming the 
research hypothesis of GNNs having better applicability to fraud detection within decentralized 
networks. 
Logistic Regression was comparatively good with a general accuracy of 87.33%. Nevertheless, 
its precision on illicit transactions was low (0.4308), and it generated a lot of false positives.

68
Although the model is still understandable and quick to train, its inability to capture multifaceted 
relations and interactions was noted. 
The Isolation Forest model completely failed to detect fraud classes with a zero illicit precision 
and recall score. Although it had a relatively good overall precision (80.09%), the failure to 
classify the minority class makes it unsuitable for real-world fraud identification. This result 
highlights the shortcomings of unsupervised anomaly detecting techniques in case of overly 
imbalanced and structurally complicated datasets. 
The Autoencoder model also lagged behind with a paltry 18.95% accuracy. While it had a high 
recall (0.9582) on illicit transfers, it had a very low precision (0.1039), and it practically 
legitimized all transactions as suspicious. This type of imbalanced prediction pattern has the 
potential to cause alarm fatigue and is unusable in practice. This demonstrates the tendency of 
the autoencoder to generalize anomalies when it has been trained on dense and unbalanced data.

4.3 DISCUSSION 
The discussion section synthesizes the results of the study with existing literature, interpreting 
the implications of the findings and drawing connections to the broader field of fraud detection 
in blockchain environments. This section aims to critically analyze how the outcomes align or 
diverge from previous research, examine the practical applicability of the findings, and suggest 
future directions for both research and implementation. 
4.3.1 Reinforcing the Value of GNNs for Fraud Detection 
One of the key goals of the research was to assess the suitability of Graph Neural Networks 
(GNNs) to fraud detection in blockchain systems. The secondary dataset analysis performance 
results attest to this goal emphatically. Of the three models used—Graph Convolutional Network

71
(GCN), Graph Attention Network (GAT), and GraphSAGE—GraphSAGE performed best with 
overall maximum accuracy (95.08%) and F1-score (0.7852) against the illicit class. 
This improved performance follows existing research that deems GNNs as apt for learning 
relations in structured data settings. Wu et al. (2020), as an example, highlights the ability of 
GNNs to learn from both node features and graph structure and are best utilized in applications 
such as cybersecurity and detecting financial fraud. The results similarly confirm the scalability 
and inductive power of GraphSAGE that has been proven to learn well from large-scale graph 
settings with minimal overfitting. 
GAT's solid performance further validates the necessity of attention mechanisms to learn 
contextually weighted representations as published by works such as Velickovic et al. (2018). 
GAT's ability to selectively pay attention to the vital node relations helped it achieve its high 
recall and accuracy on both legal and illegal transactions. GCN was somewhat weaker but still 
performed strongly and presented a standard benchmark to compare against. 
Collectively, they add to the expanding empirical evidence supporting GNNs against 
conventional flat feature-based machine learning models on graph-structured data like 
blockchain networks. They show how the employment of both feature vectors and graph 
connectivity is essential to detecting fraudulent activity often embedded in network patterns. 
4.3.2 Traditional Machine Learning Limitations 
The comparison with the existing traditional machine learning models emphasized their inability 
to handle complex and imbalanced blockchain transaction data. Although Logistic Regression 
was good enough to achieve an accuracy of 87.33%, it was lacking in precision for illicit 
transactions. This indicates a susceptibility to overfit to the majority class, a common drawback 
in the case of binary-classification tasks with extreme class imbalance. 
The Isolation Forest model was particularly ineffectual and was unable to identify a single illegal 
transaction. This highlights a fundamental weakness of anomaly detection models when used in 
domains with high levels of noise and overlapping distributions between normal and fraud. 
While such models are commonplace in conventional fraud detection, the performance in 
decentralized high-dimensional transaction graphs here indicates their usefulness in such 
domains is lessened.

72
The Autoencoder had a strong recall on illegal cases (0.9582) but had a very low precision 
(0.1039), leading to a high volume of false positives. This behavior is indicative of the model's 
failure to differentiate between genuine anomalies and real fraud in a refined, structured network. 
This is in line with previous research citing that deep unsupervised models are heavily reliant on 
finely tuned thresholds and context-aware feature selection, and as such may fail to generalize to 
different financial systems. 
These constraints imply that conventional models, as useful as they are in certain tabular areas, 
are not capable of relational reasoning to examine blockchain transactions and do not capture 
interdependencies and temporal patterns which GNNs are naturally capable of modeling. 
4.3.3 Practical Implications and Real-World Applications 
Practically speaking, the findings imply that integrating GNN-based systems in blockchain 
monitoring platforms has the ability to enhance fraud-detection capabilities by a considerable 
margin. Financial regulators, exchanges of cryptocurrencies, and blockchain analytics agencies 
are in a position to leverage the high precision and context-aware learning brought by models 
such as GraphSAGE and GAT. 
One of the main learnings is the trade-off between recall and precision. In fraud detection, false 
negatives (undetected fraud) are usually more costly than false positives (flagging legitimate 
transactions). GNNs, and especially GraphSAGE, had high recall rates but maintained decent 
precision, suggesting their practicality in applications where keeping undetected fraud low is 
paramount. 
However, deployment at scale necessitates additional considerations. GNN models are compute-
intensive and require strong infrastructure both to train and to perform inference on. In real-time 
usage, methods like graph sampling and dynamic graph update as well as hardware accelerations 
on GPUs or TPUs would be needed. 
Additionally, explainability is a concern. Although GAT does give some insight through attention 
scores, GNNs are less interpretable than rule-based or linear models overall. To support high-
stakes environments such as finance, better model clarity is essential to facilitate compliance and 
auditability.

73
4.3.4 User Perception and Cognitive Factors 
The survey data from the main dataset provide a complementary insight by investigating user 
attitudes and cognitive mappings with blockchain technology and fraud. The results highlight 
how end-users judge technical tools such as GNNs and how different variables influence their 
confidence in decentralized finance (DeFi) systems. 
Support for Hypothesis 3—that familiarity with blockchain's graph structure enhances 
confidence in the efficacy of GNNs—is particularly strong. It indicates technical competence has 
a direct effect on user confidence in sophisticated methods of fraud detection. The implication is 
that public education about blockchain's technical foundations has a direct influence on the 
uptake of safe systems. It also lends validity to open communication approaches on how AI 
models work on financial systems. 
The divergent results of Hyptheses 1 and 2 highlight the multifaceted nature of risk perception 
and human trust. The ability to comprehend blockchain does not map onto general confidence in 
DeFi across all groups of respondents, and exposure to fraud does not always decrease user 
confidence. These results align with psychological studies of user behavior in fintech, and they 
identify cognitive biases, lived experience, and media influence as drivers of perception. 
The addition of such human considerations into fraud-detection systems—via feedback loops to 
users, explainable interfaces and adaptive risk assessment—would both increase the efficacy and 
usability of AI-powered tools. 
4.3.5 Alignment with Literature and Research Contributions 
The results and interpretation are generally aligned with existing academic agreement on how 
GNNs are a leading methodology on graph-structured data tasks. Other research, such as Zhang 
et al. (2022) and Liu et al. (2021), have highlighted the increased usage of GNNs in 
cybersecurity, recommendation systems, and financial modeling. The research applies the same 
insights to the area of cryptocurrency fraud detection but through both empirical model 
assessment and user perceptions. 
The study also adds to existing research by showing the applicability of a real data set (Elliptic 
Bitcoin Transactions) and proving the effectiveness of inductive GNN models compared to both 
supervised and unsupervised conventional methods. The addition of the main survey data

74
presents a different perspective by combining the technological and human sides of fraud 
prevention. 
4.3.6 Limitations and Considerations 
Even with the positive outcomes, however, there are constraints to the research that have to be 
noted. The main dataset was comparatively small (N=20), meaning the statistical power of 
survey-based conclusions was constrained. More diverse and larger samples would yield more 
stable insights into how fraud and technology are perceived by the public. 
From a technical perspective, model performance may also be improved through hyperparameter 
adjustment, sophisticated sampling algorithms, and integrating temporal graph models to account 
for how transactions change over time—a key consideration in the case of blockchain analytics. 
Another drawback is the binary approach to classification, reducing the fraud problem to licit 
versus illicit. In fact, fraud itself is multi-dimensional and could be better addressed by multi-
class or multi-label classification systems to accommodate richer types of fraud or suspicious 
patterns. 
4.3.7 Future Research Directions 
Future research should investigate dynamic GNN models on temporal graphs like TGAT or 
DySAT to capture dynamic fraud patterns. Introducing attention-based mechanisms of 
explainability will also increase trust and uptake in regulated sectors. Investigating federated 
learning and privacy-preserving GNNs will also go hand in hand with ethical and legal practices 
in dealing with sensitive data of a financial nature. 
At the user perception level, additional research might utilize larger surveys or experiments to 
test how user confidence in AI models changes following multiple presentations of transparent 
model explanations, audit trails, or interactive fraud simulation tools.

of both precision, recall, and F1-score. 
In contrast, conventional models like Logistic Regression, Isolation Forest, and Autoencoders 
had inconsistent or weak performance. Logistic Regression was decent on recall but was poor on 
precision because it had a very high rate of false positives. Isolation Forest completely missed 
fraudulent activity, and the Autoencoder was severely prone to high sensitivity, flagging a very 
high percentage of all transactions as suspicious and making it de facto useless. These findings 
prove the essential role played by relational modeling and the shortcomings of flat feature-based 
methods in fraud identification. 
The first survey brought a human perspective to the research by showing a moderate correlation 
between familiarity with blockchain and decentralized platform trust. The survey also illustrated 
how users knowledgeable about the graph structure of blockchain data are more trusting towards 
GNNs in fraud detection. These findings are a testament to technical competence possibly 
building confidence in AI-driven cybersecurity solutions but also showing considerable 
variability as individuals differ. 
5.2 Support for Existing Literature 
The result fits neatly with the larger body of work in graph-based deep learning. Works such as 
those of Kipf & Welling (2016) and Velickovic et al. (2018) highlight the advantages of GNNs 
at learning node and graph topology properties. The work extends such results to the case of

76
detecting cryptocurrency transaction fraud and confirms the theoretical benefit of GNNs in 
networked settings. 
As in previous research, the study also confirms the constraints of anomaly detection algorithms 
such as Isolation Forest in imbalanced data. Several studies have cautioned against the 
ineffectiveness of unsupervised models under the context of fraud because of their reliance on 
statistical variance instead of context learning, as seen in the zero-recall against illicit 
transactions in the research. 
As an additional aspect from a human factor perspective, the outcomes confirm prior behavioral 
research to conclude that increased levels of technological awareness enhance confidence in 
automated systems. Several studies on financial technology adoption have consistently noted 
perceived competence and familiarity as having a favorable influence on system acceptance, a 
finding replicated first in the responses to the user survey on blockchain and GNN awareness. 
5.3 Contradictions to Existing Literature 
In contrast to general agreement with the literature, some results conflicted with previous 
expectations. One was how prior fraud exposure was associated with user confidence. The initial 
hypothesis had been that individuals with fraud experience or suspicion of fraud would indicate 
less confidence to recognize subsequent fraudulent activity. Results were however far from 
uniform—some fraud-experienced participants were still very confident, whereas others with no 
fraud experience were low in confidence. This discrepancy implies a non-linear and more 
complicated association between experience and perceived ability, possibly driven by prior 
training or by personality. 
Another difference was found in the performance of the Autoencoder. Although other research 
has found success in applying Autoencoders to fraud and cybersecurity applications, including 
because of their unsupervised anomaly identification ability, in our research the model was 
unsuccessful. Although it had good recall, it was accompanied by a steep rise in the number of 
false positives and a resulting very low overall accuracy and dependability. This failure might 
result from the excessive dimensionality and unbalanced classes in the blockchain dataset, 
whereby minor differences can saturate reconstruction error thresholds.

77
5.4 Limitations and Challenges 
There were numerous limitations and challenges to the study. The first was the small sample size 
of the main dataset to limit the survey analysis's statistical power. Since there were just 50 
participants, the outcomes are better understood as indicative exploratory trends and not 
conclusive answers. Having a larger and more demographically varied sample could facilitate 
more generalizable conclusions. Secondly, the secondary dataset's class imbalance was a 
problem to deal with during training. Since merely 8.8% of the transactions were marked as 
illicit, models were prone to become biased towards the majority class. Although class weight 
and stratified sampling alleviated it to a certain extent, oversample methods such as SMOTE 
were incapable of being utilized because the graph structure restricted additional performance 
augmentations. 
The training requirements of GNNs also imposed constraints. GraphSAGE, GAT, and GCN 
models were time and memory-intensive to train and restricted the level of hyperparameter 
tuning. This could impact reproducibility and scalability and was a concern in institutions with 
less powerful computing environments. 
One of the limitations was also the lack of temporal dynamics. While the data had timestamps, 
the models were static and did not account for transaction sequence or even time-varying graph 
structures. This restricted the capacity to identify patterns concerning evolving fraudulent 
activities. In the future, research could look into using temporal GNN variants to account for 
time-sensitive fraud indicators. 
Finally, the unexplained nature of GNN models is a problem. Financial and security issues 
necessitate high levels of transparency but most GNNs are black boxes. Although attention 
mechanisms in GAT are somewhat interpretable, full tools like GNNExplainer were not 
incorporated in the research. Model transparency is a necessity to deploy the model in real-life 
scenarios. 
5.5 Final Thoughts 
This study makes a valuable addition to the academic and applied fields of fraud detection in 
blockchain systems. The better performance of GNNs, and especially GraphSAGE, demonstrates

78
the necessity of models with the ability to utilize both transaction features and interconnection 
patterns of blockchain networks. The study also highlights the shortcomings of conventional or 
unsupervised models to deal with sophisticated, unbalanced, and graph-structured data. 
The inclusion of user perception introduces a welcome additional perspective, illustrating how 
technical system success is also shaped by user knowledge and confidence. Becoming ever more 
relevant as blockchain and DeFi systems expand, it will become a growing concern to make 
certain that users understand and have confidence in the tools safeguarding them. Although 
difficulties are still present—data imbalance, computational expense, and interpretability among 
them—the research confirms the promise of graph-based AI as a core enabling technology for 
robust, smart financial systems. The goal in the coming research must be to advance scalability, 
transparency, and real-time responsiveness to guarantee that financial systems are both accurate 
and trusted and available to all.

import torch 
import torch.nn as nn 
import torch.nn.functional as F 
from torch_geometric.nn import GCNConv, GATConv, SAGEConv 
 
class GCN(nn.Module): 
    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden_channels, out_channels): 
        super(GCN, self).__init__() 
        self.conv1 = GCNConv(in_channels, hidden_channels) 
        self.conv2 = GCNConv(hidden_channels, out_channels) 
 
    def forward(self, x, edge_index):

96
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index)) 
        x = F.dropout(x, p=0.5, training=self.training) 
        x = self.conv2(x, edge_index) 
        return x 
 
class GAT(nn.Module): 
    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden_channels, out_channels): 
        super(GAT, self).__init__() 
        self.conv1 = GATConv(in_channels, hidden_channels, heads=4, concat=True) 
        self.conv2 = GATConv(hidden_channels * 4, out_channels, heads=1, concat=False) 
 
    def forward(self, x, edge_index): 
        x = F.elu(self.conv1(x, edge_index)) 
        x = F.dropout(x, p=0.5, training=self.training) 
        x = self.conv2(x, edge_index) 
        return x 
 
class GraphSAGE(nn.Module): 
    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden_channels, out_channels): 
        super(GraphSAGE, self).__init__() 
        self.conv1 = SAGEConv(in_channels, hidden_channels)

97
        self.conv2 = SAGEConv(hidden_channels, out_channels) 
 
    def forward(self, x, edge_index): 
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index)) 
        x = F.dropout(x, p=0.5, training=self.training) 
        x = self.conv2(x, edge_index) 
        return x

# Training Function 
def train(model, data, optimizer, criterion): 
    model.train() 
    optimizer.zero_grad() 
    out = model(data.x, data.edge_index) 
    loss = criterion(out[data.train_mask], data.y[data.train_mask]) 
    loss.backward() 
    optimizer.step() 
    return loss.item()

# Run GNN model 
def run_model(model_class, model_name, data, class_weights=None): 
    print(f"\n--- Training {model_name.upper()} ---") 
    model = model_class(data.num_node_features, 64, 2) 
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.005, weight_decay=5e-4) 
    if class_weights is not None: 
        criterion = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss(weight=class_weights) 
    else: 
        criterion = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss() 
 
    for epoch in range(1, 101): 
        loss = train(model, data, optimizer, criterion) 
        if epoch % 10 == 0: 
            print(f"Epoch {epoch:03d} | Loss: {loss:.4f}") 
 
    evaluate(model, data, split='test')
